mainText:
### A New Wild West in Jackson, Louisiana

There was a time when Louisiana was considered part of "The Wild West." Men came here from the colony states looking for frontier opportunity, which really meant steep upsides for the price of mortal risk. These were not your reliable family men; they were West-bound opportunists and snake oil salesmen. These characters resemble Daniel Plainview from *There Will Be Blood* and the land-stealing villains from *Chinatown*. It's the sometimes ignored shadow aspect of the loner cowboy hero, revealed by considering what exactly John Wayne was always running from.

In the early 1800s, a man named John Horton came to Louisiana with his business partner looking to strike big. My parish, Feliciana, had recently been sold to the U.S. by the Spanish. The courthouse was established in St. Francisville, bordering the Mississippi River and the westernmost edge of the parish. Those living on the east side with legal matters would have to spend a fortnight trekking through un-European forest swamps and rolling hills, often encountering creeks filled with quicksand.

These were the circumstances observed by Horton, who quickly made connections with local power brokers. He learned that they planned to erect a new town in the geographic center of Feliciana and relocate the courthouse there. Horton wanted in.

Horton acquired the assessor’s map and determined the exact center of the parish, which happened to be on the bluffs of what would later be known as Thompson Creek. He contacted the landowners and struck a bargain to purchase large swaths of their property. Soon, a town was founded there, and he sold parts of it to the state for a steep markup, keeping other portions to explore further opportunities. He was already rich.

Many friends of Horton who later settled in his town had fought in the Battle of New Orleans. They named their new town after their tough old general, Andrew Jackson, who was known for his daring nature. The people in Jackson were not focused on establishing churches and families; they sought opportunity and were told they could hit it big. As second-comers, they often resorted to poker and other less reputable pursuits.

Various questionable businesses proliferated in Jackson: pool halls, gambling dens, and bars. The rowdy Jacksonites were so infamous that people preferred the trek to St. Francisville for court matters over visiting Jackson. This led politicians to split Feliciana in half, designating St. Francisville as the courthouse for the western side, while Clinton took the eastern side. Jackson was left without representation, and Horton, ultimately ruined, sued the judge for $50,000, which is almost two million dollars today, but lost. He died poor and far from his family.

On Main Street in Jackson stands the largest live oak, marked with a plaque that reads "John Horton, founder of Jackson." He continues to be the guiding spirit of the town, whether recognized by locals or not. This history reflects a pattern: A significant Southern college was established here before relocating due to political issues; the largest and most beautiful asylum campus was once here but was defunded by corrupt Louisiana politicians; even a *Twilight* sequel was filmed here, but the local studio's reputation drove producers away.

Our opportunism has yielded unlikely benefits, and that lack of restraint has often resulted in overreaching.

If I were promoting Jackson for tourism, I would likely fail. Instead, I want to convey that Jackson is not an aberration to avoid but a pure embodiment of the American spirit, a place ripe for redemption. We are all John Horton, searching for our big opportunity, and here we can correct his mistakes.

Growing up in Jackson, my father, an entrepreneur himself, always seemed to be running West, perhaps dreaming of a frontier before I even knew who John Horton was. I eventually found myself in California, living on Sunset Boulevard, chasing the shadows of the last hyper-frontiers in Silicon Valley, willing to take risks for potential rewards. That was where I met my wife, but I also found myself contemplating the outcome of such pursuits.

Strangely, I had a dream urging me to move back to Jackson, and eventually, my wife and I did. I cannot explain it, but many others have followed this intuition, spending millions to renovate historic homes, theaters
